title: Web Development Basics for Students: HTML, CSS, and Beyond
lead: Building a simple webpage is satisfying. HTML structures content, CSS makes it look good. Students can see results quickly, which makes it a great entry point.

sections.title: HTML: Structure First
content: Headings, paragraphs, lists, links, and images. Learn the basic tags. A simple page with your name, a bio, and a few links is a solid first project.

sections.title: CSS: Make It Look Good
content: Colors, fonts, layout. Start with inline or internal CSS, then move to external stylesheets. Flexbox and Grid come later—basic styling first.

sections.title: Add Interactivity With JavaScript
content: Buttons that change text, forms that validate, simple animations. JavaScript brings pages to life. Learn the basics after HTML and CSS feel comfortable.

sections.title: Resources
content: Free editors like VS Code, browsers' developer tools, and sites like CodePen for quick experiments. No fancy setup needed to get started.

cta.title: Build Skills With Expert Guidance
cta.text: GuruForU tutors can guide your child through web development basics.

faq.question: What should students learn first in web development?
faq.answer: HTML for structure—headings, paragraphs, lists, links, images. A simple page with a name, bio, and links is a solid first project.

faq.question: When should students add CSS?
faq.answer: After basic HTML. Start with colors, fonts, layout. Inline or internal CSS first, then external stylesheets. Flexbox and Grid come later.

faq.question: What does JavaScript add to a webpage?
faq.answer: Interactivity: buttons that change text, form validation, simple animations. Learn JavaScript basics after HTML and CSS feel comfortable.

faq.question: What tools do students need for web development?
faq.answer: A free editor like VS Code, browser developer tools, and sites like CodePen for quick experiments. No fancy setup needed.
